Do NOT report "Mathlib lacks X" and stop. Before filing "Infrastructure missing":

A long dispatch may be auto-backgrounded with a task ID — that's fine; wait for that task's result before acting. **Do NOT use the `Monitor` tool to wait** — it is non-blocking (returns immediately, "keep working"), so your turn ends and the subagents are abandoned mid-run. **Do NOT use foreground `sleep` / `until … ; do sleep … ; done`** — the harness blocks `sleep`. To run independent subagents **in parallel**, put all their dispatches in **one Bash call**, each backgrounded with `&`, ending with `wait` — e.g. `python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py --name A … & python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py --name B … & wait` — which blocks until all finish (the whole wave as one blocking dispatch). Serialize only subagents that write the **same file** (separate sequential calls). The rule never changes: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required.
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `ARCHON_MEMORY.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read (edits via `archon discuss`) |
57	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
58	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
60	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
61	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
62	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
65	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
66	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
67	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
68	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
69	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
70	| `TO_USER.md` | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | read |
71	
72	### Subagent permissions
73	
74	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
75	
76	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
77	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
78	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
79	
80	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
81	
82	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper.
83	
84	## Protected Declarations
85	
86	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
87	
88	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
89	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
90	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
91	
92	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
93	
94	Two semantic markers:
95	
96	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
97	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
98	
99	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
100	
101	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake build <module>` (it uses `lake build`, not `lake env lean <file>`, so dependencies are built and missing-import failures aren't spurious), and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents should NOT add or remove `\leanok` as routine bookkeeping — leave it to the sync. The one exception is the review agent: if it is **certain** the sync got a marker wrong (a clearly-finished proof left unmarked, or a marker present on something incomplete), it may manually correct it, but must explicitly surface and justify that override in its summary (see `.archon/prompts/review.md`).
102	
103	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
104	
105	## User Interaction
106	
107	The user provides hints in two places:
108	
109	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
110	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
111	
112	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
113	
114	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
115	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true or important at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
116	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
117	
118	## Agent Roles
119	
120	### Plan Agent
121	
122	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
123	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
124	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
125	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
126	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
127	- Curate `ARCHON_MEMORY.md` — the condensed, cross-iteration knowledge file injected (read-only) into every agent's prompt. Keep only durable facts that would surprise an agent reading the code fresh (dead-end tactics, files not to touch, Mathlib gap coordinates, protected invariants). Hard limits: ~10 bullets / ~600 chars; prune before adding. Do NOT duplicate what's obvious from the code or PROGRESS.md.
128	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
129	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
130	
131	### Prover Agent
132	
133	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
134	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
135	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
136	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
137	- A prover lane ends by writing its task result and exiting normally. **Do not kill Lean, Lake, shell, or sibling agent processes from inside the prover lane.** Do not run `pkill`, `killall`, regex-based process cleanup, or background-verification cleanup. If a verification command is still running, wait for its result or report that it is still running; process lifetime is owned by the Archon runner/orchestrator.
138	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
139	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
140	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
141	
142	### Subagents
143	
144	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
145	
146	### Review Agent
147	
148	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
149	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
150	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
151	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
152	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Normally leave `\leanok` to the deterministic `sync_leanok` phase; only override a marker the sync got demonstrably wrong, and justify it in your summary.
153	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
154	
155	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
156	
157	These run automatically between phases:
158	
159	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
160	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
161	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
162

35	variable {D : Type u} [Category.{u, u} D] {R₀ : Dᵒᵖ ⥤ CommRingCat.{u}}

38	(restricted) unit `restr X 𝟙_ ⟶ restr X 𝟙_` are identified `R₀(X)`-linearly with `R₀(X)` itself,

42	noncomputable def unitDualSectionEquiv (X : Dᵒᵖ) :
43	    letI := internalHomObjModule X.unop
44	      (𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)))
45	      (𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)))
46	    (restr X.unop (𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat))) ⟶
47	        restr X.unop (𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat))))
48	      ≃ₗ[(R₀.obj (op X.unop) : Type u)] (R₀.obj (op X.unop) : Type u) := by
49	  letI := internalHomObjModule X.unop
50	    (𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)))
51	    (𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)))
52	  exact
53	    { toFun := fun φ =>
54	        evalLin (𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat))) X φ
55	          (1 : ((R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat).obj X : Type u))
56	      map_add' := fun φ φ' => rfl
57	      map_smul' := fun c φ => by

74	            (restr X.unop
75	              (𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)))) r).app

85	    PresheafOfModules.dual (R₀ := R₀)

87	      ≅ 𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)) :=
88	  PresheafOfModules.isoMk (fun X => (unitDualSectionEquiv X).toModuleIso)
89	    (fun {X Y} f => by
90	      refine ModuleCat.hom_ext (LinearMap.ext fun φ => ?_)
91	      change evalLin (𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat))) Y
92	            ((PresheafOfModules.dual
93	              (𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)))).map f φ)
94	            (1 : ((R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat).obj Y : Type u))
95	          = ((𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat))).map f).hom
96	              (evalLin (𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat))) X φ
97	                (1 : ((R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat).obj X : Type u)))
98	      have key := PresheafOfModules.naturality_apply
99	        (φ : restr X.unop (𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat))) ⟶
100	          restr X.unop (𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat))))

102	        (1 : ((R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat).obj X : Type u))
103	      have hrm : (restr X.unop
104	            (𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)))).map
105	          (Over.homMk f.unop : Over.mk f.unop ⟶ Over.mk (𝟙 X.unop)).op
106	          = (𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat))).map f := rfl
107	      rw [hrm] at key
108	      erw [PresheafOfModules.unit_map_one] at key
109	      have hAB : (op (Over.mk (𝟙 Y.unop ≫ f.unop)) : (Over X.unop)ᵒᵖ) = op (Over.mk f.unop) :=
110	        congrArg op (congrArg Over.mk (Category.id_comp f.unop))
111	      have homAppHEq : ∀ {A B : (Over X.unop)ᵒᵖ} (_ : A = B), HEq (φ.app A) (φ.app B) := by

113	      have hdt : evalLin (𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat))) Y
114	          ((PresheafOfModules.dual
115	            (𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)))).map f φ)
116	          = (φ.app (op (Over.mk f.unop))).hom :=
117	        congrArg ModuleCat.Hom.hom (eq_of_heq (homAppHEq hAB))
118	      exact (DFunLike.congr_fun hdt _).trans key)

278	    -- β-compatibility (iter-303): `β` is the open-immersion structure ring iso `(f.appIso).inv`,
279	    -- so post-composing it with `(f.appIso P).hom` is the identity on `𝒪_X(f''ᵁP)`.  This is the
280	    -- load-bearing ring identity that collapses the double `restrictScalars` in the reverse
281	    -- component (`?collapse`); it is FALSE for an arbitrary `β`, hence supplied as a hypothesis and
282	    -- discharged at the unique caller (`sliceDualTransport.invFun`) via `Iso.hom_inv_id`.
283	    (hβ : ∀ (P : TopologicalSpace.Opens ↥Y),
284	        ((β.app (op P)).hom).comp ((Scheme.Hom.appIso f P).hom.hom) = RingHom.id _)

286	    (((PresheafOfModules.pushforward β).obj M.val.dual).obj V : Type u) := by
287	  refine { app := fun W'' => ?_, naturality := ?_ }
288	  · -- app component at `W''` (over `fV`).  `W' := (unop W'').left ≤ fV`; `P := f⁻¹ᵁ W'`.
289	    -- The down-set facts are established (axiom-clean); the morphism itself is the documented
290	    -- residual below.
291	    set W' := (unop W'').left with hW'

293	    have hPV : f ⁻¹ᵁ W' ≤ unop V :=
294	      le_trans ((TopologicalSpace.Opens.map f.base).monotone hW'fV)
295	        (le_of_eq (f.preimage_image_eq (unop V)))
296	    have he : f ''ᵁ (f ⁻¹ᵁ W') = W' := by
297	      rw [Scheme.Hom.image_preimage_eq_opensRange_inf]
298	      exact inf_eq_right.mpr (hW'fV.trans (f.image_le_opensRange (unop V)))
299	    -- **app component — CLOSED axiom-clean (iter-303).**  The X-slice mirror of the forward
300	    -- `toFun`, conjugated across the propositional preimage round-trip `he : f''ᵁ(f⁻¹ᵁ W') = W'`.
301	    -- It is the four-leg composite (all legs concrete):
302	    --   (1) `M.val.map (eqToHom (op he.symm))` : source relabel `M.val(W') ⟶ restr_ρ M.val(fP)`
303	    --       (SEMILINEAR — codomain restricted along `ρ = X.ringCatSheaf.map (eqToHom (op he.symm))`,
304	    --       crossing the `𝒪_X(W') ↔ 𝒪_X(fP)` fiber);
305	    --   (2) `restrictScalars ρ |>.map (?collapse ≫ core)` transports the in-fiber-`fP` core:
306	    --       `?collapse` (the double-restrict collapse `M.val(fP) ≅ restrictScalars (f.appIso P).hom
307	    --       (restrictScalars (β.app P) (M.val fP))` via `restrictScalarsId'App` + `restrictScalarsComp'App`
308	    --       fed the ring identity `hβ (f⁻¹ᵁ W')`), and `core` (legs (3) ψ-reindex `restrictScalars
309	    --       (f.appIso P).hom |>.map (ψ.app …)` + (4) codomain unit swap `dualUnitRingSwapHom f P`);
310	    --   (3) `unitRelabelSwap (op he.symm)` : the codomain unit transport `restrictScalars ρ 𝟙_X(fP)
311	    --       ⟶ 𝟙_X(W')` (`inv ε` of the relabel, the new top-level helper).
312	    -- The cross-fiber transport (a single `≫`-chain cannot express it — the relabel is semilinear)
313	    -- is realised by applying the functor `restrictScalars ρ` to the in-fiber-`fP` core.
314	    -- **core (legs 3+4): VERIFIED well-formed in fiber `𝒪_X(fP)` (iter-303).**  The ψ-reindex
315	    -- `restrictScalars (f.appIso P).hom ∘ ψ.app` post-composed with the codomain unit swap
316	    -- `dualUnitRingSwapHom f P` assembles into
317	    --   `core : restrictScalars (f.appIso P).hom ((pushforward β M.val)(P)) ⟶ 𝟙_X(fP)`,
318	    -- a morphism of `ModuleCat 𝒪_X(fP)`.  (NB: the leg-3 target `restrictScalars (f.appIso P).hom
319	    -- ((restr V 𝟙_Y)-section)` DID defeq-unify with leg-4's `restrictScalars (f.appIso P).hom
320	    -- (𝟙_ (ModuleCat 𝒪_Y(P)))` — the unit-spelling reconciles here, exactly as in the closed
321	    -- forward `toFun`.)
322	    have core := (ModuleCat.restrictScalars (Scheme.Hom.appIso f (f ⁻¹ᵁ W')).hom.hom).map
323	        (ψ.app (op (Over.mk (homOfLE hPV)))) ≫ dualUnitRingSwapHom f (f ⁻¹ᵁ W')
324	    -- **Cross-fiber transport — CLOSED (iter-303).**  The goal lives in `ModuleCat 𝒪_X(W')` but
325	    -- `core` lives in `ModuleCat 𝒪_X(fP)` (`fP = f''ᵁf⁻¹ᵁW'`, propositionally `= W'` via `he`, but
326	    -- the section RINGS `𝒪_X(W')` / `𝒪_X(fP)` are only propositionally equal).  The source relabel
327	    -- `M.val(W') ⟶ M.val(fP)` is `M.val.map (eqToHom (op he.symm))` — SEMILINEAR, landing in
328	    -- `restrictScalars (X.ringCatSheaf.map (eqToHom …))`; combined with the source double-restrict
329	    -- collapse `restrictScalars (f.appIso P).hom ∘ restrictScalars (β.app P) ≅ restrictScalars 𝟙
330	    -- ≅ id` (ring identity `hβ (f⁻¹ᵁ W')`: `(β.app P).hom ∘ (f.appIso P).hom.hom = 𝟙_{𝒪_X(fP)}`,
331	    -- collapsed by `ModuleCat.restrictScalarsComp'App` + `restrictScalarsId'App`).  A single
332	    -- `≫`-chain in one `ModuleCat` cannot express this — the relabel crosses ring fibers — so `core`
333	    -- is conjugated across the `𝒪_X(fP) ↔ 𝒪_X(W')` fiber by applying the functor
334	    -- `restrictScalars (X.ringCatSheaf.map (eqToHom (op he.symm)))` to `?collapse ≫ core` (per memory

1	# Analogy: naturality of a sectionwise transport `legA ≫ inv ε` over a thin poset
2	
3	## Mode
4	cross-domain-inspiration
5	
6	## Slug
7	dualnat006
8	
9	## Iteration
10	006
11	
12	## Structural problem (abstracted)
13	A family of morphisms `T_V : A(V) ⟶ B(V)` indexed by a thin poset, each `T_V = legA_V ≫ (inv ε)_V`,
14	must satisfy a naturality square for every `g : V ⟶ W`. Leg-B = `inv ε`, where `ε` is the
15	lax-monoidal unit comparison of a change-of-base functor (`restrictScalars`). The pointwise
16	reduction (`ext z; simp [ε-as-ringmap]`) times out at `whnf` because it forces the deep `inv ε`
17	composite through `whnf` on every section element. We want naturality WITHOUT that reduction.
18	
19	## Failed approaches (from directive)
20	- `ext z; simp [pushforward/restrictScalars]; exact (φ.naturality …) z`: whnf heartbeat timeout.
21	- `subsingleton` on the OUTER isoMk square: only valid for dual-valued (unit-target) codomain.
22	- hand-pasting the four legs through the restriction `.map` via `erw`: never assembles.
23	
24	## Analogues found
25	
26	### Analogue: `CategoryTheory.IsIso.inv_comp_eq` + `CategoryTheory.CommSq.horiz_inv`
27	- **Domain**: category theory (general iso calculus). `Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Iso`,
28	  `Mathlib.CategoryTheory.CommSq`.
29	- **Same structural problem there**: a commuting square one of whose edges is an isomorphism's
30	  INVERSE. `CommSq.horiz_inv`: `CommSq f.hom g h i.hom → CommSq f.inv h g i.inv` — a square with
31	  iso horizontal edges stays commuting when both are inverted (and flipped). `IsIso.inv_comp_eq`:
32	  `inv α ≫ f = g ↔ f = α ≫ g`.
33	- **Technique**: never apply `inv ε` to an element. Rotate the `inv ε` edge across the equation /
34	  invert the whole square so the goal becomes the FORWARD `ε` square. The forward square is plain
35	  `ε`-naturality, closed by a coherence lemma. Entirely morphism-level — no `ext z`, no
36	  `dualUnitRingSwap_apply`, so `whnf` is never invoked on `inv ε`.
37	- **Mapping to project**: in `sliceDualTransport.naturality` (DualInverse.lean:547-556) and
38	  `sliceDualTransportInv.naturality` (DualInverse.lean:398-410), `dualUnitRingSwap = inv (ε …)` is
39	  the iso edge. Replace `ext z; simp only [..., dualUnitRingSwap_apply]` with
40	  `apply PresheafOfModules.hom_ext` then `rw [IsIso.inv_comp_eq]` (or `CommSq.horiz_inv` on the
41	  pre-built forward square), turning the goal into the forward-`ε` naturality square that
42	  `φ.naturality i.op` (already `hφ`) feeds.
43	- **Porting cost**: LOW. No new infra; `isIso_ε_restrictScalars_appIso` already gives the `IsIso`.
44	- **Verdict**: ANALOGUE_FOUND.
45	
46	### Analogue: `CategoryTheory.NatTrans.IsMonoidal.unit` (`.unit_assoc`)
47	- **Domain**: monoidal category theory. `Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.NaturalTransformation`.
48	- **Same structural problem there**: for a monoidal natural transformation `τ : F₁ ⟶ F₂` between
49	  lax-monoidal functors, the unit axiom `ε F₁ ≫ τ.app 𝟙_C = ε F₂` (assoc form:
50	  `ε F₁ ≫ τ.app 𝟙 ≫ h = ε F₂ ≫ h`). This IS the "pointwise ε-commutation equation" the project is
51	  stuck on, stated as a single coherence axiom.
52	- **Technique**: recognize the leg-B family as the unit cell of a monoidal nat-trans between the
53	  two `restrictScalars`-composites bridged by the `appIso`-naturality square; discharge by
54	  `IsMonoidal.unit` — one lemma, no reduction.
55	- **Mapping to project**: the ε-commutation crux (the equation that 30+ iters could not close) is
56	  the `unit` field of a `NatTrans.IsMonoidal` instance for the relabel comparison. ε formula at the
57	  carrier: `ModuleCat.restrictScalars_η : ε(restrictScalars f) r = f r`
58	  (`Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Monoidal.Adjunction`).
59	- **Porting cost**: LOW-MED. Need to exhibit the monoidal-nat-trans `IsMonoidal` instance for the
60	  `(f.appIso V).inv` family; the unit axiom is then the target lemma rather than a hand proof.
61	- **Verdict**: ANALOGUE_FOUND.
62	
63	### Analogue: `ModuleCat.restrictScalarsComp` + `RingCat.moduleCatRestrictScalarsPseudofunctor`
64	- **Domain**: change-of-rings / 2-category theory. `Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.ChangeOfRings`,
65	  `Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Pseudofunctor`.
66	- **Same structural problem there**: `restrictScalars` assembles into a PSEUDOFUNCTOR
67	  `RingCatᵒᵖ → Cat`; its `mapComp` 2-cell is exactly `restrictScalarsComp (g f) ≅ comp …`. All the
68	  ε/restriction coherence over a diagram of ring maps is the pseudofunctor's `mapComp`/`mapId`/`map₂`.
69	- **Technique**: the naturality the project wants over `Opens Y` is the IMAGE of the `appIso`
70	  naturality square (`Scheme.Hom.appIso_inv_naturality`) under this pseudofunctor — consume the
71	  packaged `restrictScalarsComp`/`restrictScalarsId'` isos (the project ALREADY uses
72	  `restrictScalarsComp'App`/`restrictScalarsId'App` in `sliceDualTransportInv.collapse`!) instead of
73	  reducing pointwise.
74	- **Mapping to project**: glue the forward-`ε` square (after rotation via analogue #1) using
75	  `restrictScalarsComp'App` for the two ε-legs at `V` and `W`, intertwined by the `appIso`-naturality
76	  ring identity (the project's `Scheme.Hom.appIso_inv_naturality`, already used in `map_smul'`).
77	- **Porting cost**: MED. The coherence lemmas exist; assembling the square is bookkeeping.
78	- **Verdict**: ANALOGUE_FOUND.
79	
80	### Analogue: `CategoryTheory.conjugateEquiv_comp` / `mateEquiv_vcomp`
81	- **Domain**: adjunction / mate calculus. `Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Adjunction.Mates`.
82	- **Same structural problem there**: conjugate/mate of a natural transformation respects vertical
83	  composition; the mate of a commuting square commutes automatically.
84	- **Technique**: cast leg-B (`inv ε` of `restrictScalars`) as the conjugate of the identity under
85	  the `extendScalars ⊣ restrictScalars` adjunction; mate naturality is then free.
86	- **Mapping to project**: would require exhibiting the `extendRestrictScalarsAdj` adjunctions at
87	  every section and casting the transport as a conjugate — heavy.
88	- **Porting cost**: HIGH. Adjunction scaffolding per section; overkill vs analogues #1-#3.
89	- **Verdict**: PARTIAL_ANALOGUE.
90	
91	## Top suggestion
92	Try analogue #1 (`IsIso.inv_comp_eq` / `CommSq.horiz_inv`) FIRST, glued with #3. Concretely, in
93	`DualInverse.lean` rewrite both `naturality` holes (`sliceDualTransport` ~L547, `sliceDualTransportInv`
94	~L398) to stay at the morphism level: `apply PresheafOfModules.hom_ext; intro W`, then `rw
95	[IsIso.inv_comp_eq]` (with `haveI := isIso_ε_restrictScalars_appIso f _`) to push the `inv ε`
96	(`dualUnitRingSwap`) edge to the RHS, converting the goal into the FORWARD `ε`-naturality square.
97	Close that square by gluing `hφ := φ.naturality i.op` (already in hand) to the ε-leg via
98	`ModuleCat.restrictScalars_η` + `restrictScalarsComp'App`. This removes the `dualUnitRingSwap_apply`
99	rewrite that forces `inv ε` through `whnf` per element — the documented cause of the heartbeat
100	timeout. First file to touch: `AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate/DualInverse.lean`.
101	
102	## Discarded
103	- Mate calculus full port: overlaps directive's general "build adjunctions by hand" cost; HIGH.
104	- `subsingleton`: directive failed-approach #2; only the outer dual-valued square.
105	- Reading `restrictScalars` ε pointwise via `dualUnitRingSwap_apply`: IS the failed whnf approach #1.

4595	  induces an isomorphism of duals in the reverse direction.
4596	\end{definition}
4597	
4598	\begin{lemma}
4599	
4600	[The per-section dual transport \(\mathtt{sliceDualTransport}\)]
4601	  \label{lem:slice_dual_transport}
4602	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sliceDualTransport}
4603	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv, def:dual_unit_ring_swap, lem:isiso_eps_restrictscalars_appiso, def:dual_unit_iso_gen, lem:image_preimage_of_le, def:dual_unit_ring_swap_hom, lem:isiso_eps_restrictscalars_appiso_hom, lem:slice_dual_transport_inv, def:dual_unit_ring_swap_inv, lem:dual_unit_ring_swap_comp_inv, lem:dual_unit_ring_swap_inv_comp}
4604	  Let \(f : Y \hookrightarrow X\) be an open immersion of schemes and let
4605	  \(M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). Write \(\alpha\) for the open-immersion
4606	  structure-presheaf natural transformation with components
4607	  \(\alpha_U = (f.\mathtt{appIso}\,U).\mathrm{inv}\), a \emph{\(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)}-level
4608	  ring isomorphism, and \(\beta = \mathtt{whiskerRight}\,\alpha\,
4609	  (\mathtt{forget}_2\,\mathtt{CommRingCat}\,\mathtt{RingCat})\) for its
4610	  \(\mathtt{RingCat}\)-level shadow, with component
4611	  \(\beta_V \colon \mathcal{O}_Y(V) \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_X(fV)\)
4612	  (\(fV := f.\mathtt{opensFunctor}(V)\)). Then for every open \(V \subseteq Y\) there is an
4613	  \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-linear isomorphism
4614	  \[
4615	    \bigl((\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta).\mathtt{obj}\,(\mathtt{dual}\,M.\mathtt{val})\bigr)(V)
4616	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
4617	    \bigl(\mathtt{dual}\,((\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta).\mathtt{obj}\,M.\mathtt{val})\bigr)(V),
4618	  \]
4619	  realised as a single \(\mathtt{LinearEquiv.toModuleIso}\) that packages \emph{both} leg (A)
4620	  --- the slice-Hom base-change reindexing across \(f.\mathtt{opensFunctor}\) --- and leg (B)
4621	  --- the unit codomain ring-iso swap. The naturality of the section family in \(W\) has
4622	  \emph{two} distinct parts: the underlying base-morphism uniqueness in
4623	  \((\mathtt{Over}\,V.\mathrm{unop})^{\mathrm{op}}\) is \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.elim}\) (a thin
4624	  poset has at most one inclusion between objects), but the accompanying equation of
4625	  \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-module maps is a genuine, separate obligation: it is the
4626	  \(\varepsilon\)-naturality of \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\) along the structure-ring iso
4627	  \(\beta_W\), which \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.elim}\) does \emph{not} discharge.
4628	\end{lemma}
4629	
4630	\begin{proof}
4631	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv, def:dual_unit_ring_swap, lem:isiso_eps_restrictscalars_appiso, def:dual_unit_iso_gen, lem:image_preimage_of_le, def:dual_unit_ring_swap_hom, lem:isiso_eps_restrictscalars_appiso_hom, lem:slice_dual_transport_inv, def:dual_unit_ring_swap_inv, lem:dual_unit_ring_swap_comp_inv, lem:dual_unit_ring_swap_inv_comp}
4632	  The isomorphism is produced from an \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-linear equivalence between the
4633	  left-hand module --- the \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-module inherited from
4634	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta\) --- and the right-hand \(\mathtt{internalHomObjModule}\),
4635	  assembled via \(\mathtt{LinearEquiv.toModuleIso}\), with both module structures
4636	  identified explicitly.
4637	
4638	  \emph{Leg (A): the slice-Hom reindexing, built categorically.} A dual section
4639	  \(\varphi\) of the left-hand value is a morphism \(\mathtt{restr}_{fV}\,M.\mathtt{val} \to
4640	  \mathtt{restr}_{fV}\,\mathbf{1}_X\) over \(\mathtt{Over}\,(fV)\). Its image is the dual
4641	  section over \(\mathtt{Over}\,V\) whose component at \(W \le V\) (with image
4642	  \(fW := f.\mathtt{opensFunctor}(W)\)) is the categorical image
4643	  \[
4644	    (\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\beta_W).\mathrm{map}\,
4645	      \bigl(\varphi_{(\mathtt{Over.mk}\,(f.\mathtt{opensFunctor}(W \le V)))}\bigr),
4646	  \]
4647	  i.e.\ the component of \(\varphi\) at the \(f\)-image of \(W\), reindexed across
4648	  \(f.\mathtt{opensFunctor}\) by restriction of scalars along \(\beta_W\), and then
4649	  post-composed with the leg-(B) codomain swap below.
4650	
4651	  \emph{Leg (B): the unit codomain ring-iso swap, via the lax-monoidal unit \(\varepsilon\).}
4652	  The codomain of the reindexed component is the restriction of scalars of the unit section,
4653	  which must be carried to the unit section over \(Y\). This is exactly the inverse of the
4654	  lax-monoidal unit comparison of the restriction-of-scalars functor,
4655	  \[
4656	    \mathtt{codomainMap} \;:=\;
4657	      \mathtt{inv}\bigl(\varepsilon(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,g)\bigr),
4658	      \qquad g := (f.\mathtt{appIso}\,W).\mathrm{inv}.\mathrm{hom},
4659	  \]
4660	  (\cref{def:dual_unit_ring_swap})
4661	  where \(g\) is taken at the \emph{\(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)} level. The unit \(\varepsilon\)
4662	  is invertible because \(g\) is a ring isomorphism
4663	  (\cref{lem:isiso_eps_restrictscalars_appiso}),
4664	  so \(\varepsilon(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,g)\) is an isomorphism. The presheaf-level
4665	  identification underlying the unit handling is the dual-of-unit isomorphism for the unit
4666	  section (\cref{def:dual_unit_iso_gen}).
4667	
4668	  \emph{Inverse.} The inverse reindexing depends on a set-theoretic down-set fact: because
4669	  \(f\) is an open immersion, \(fV \subseteq \mathrm{range}\,f\), so every open
4670	  \(W'' \le fV\) already lies in the image of \(f.\mathtt{opensFunctor}\), namely
4671	  \(W'' = f.\mathtt{opensFunctor}(f^{-1}W'')\). This is the open-image down-set coverage of an
4672	  \(\mathtt{IsOpenImmersion}\): an open contained in the (open) image of the immersion is the
4673	  functor-image of its own preimage. The project records it as the down-set identity
4674	  \(\iota_!(\iota^{-1}V) = V\) for \(V \le W\) (the helper
4675	  \(\mathtt{image\_preimage\_of\_le}\) (\cref{lem:image_preimage_of_le})),
4676	  and it is exactly the lemma the inverse reindexing relies on to define the components on the
4677	  whole down-set of \(fV\).
4678	
4679	  With this in hand the inverse \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Hom}\) mirrors the forward
4680	  component formula, with two changes: it uses \((f.\mathtt{appIso}\,W'').\mathrm{hom}\) in
4681	  place of its inverse, and its \(\varepsilon\)-codomain swap is
4682	  \(\mathtt{dualUnitRingSwapHom} = \mathtt{inv}\bigl(\varepsilon(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,
4683	  (f.\mathtt{appIso}\,W'').\mathrm{hom})\bigr)\) (\cref{def:dual_unit_ring_swap_hom})
4684	  --- that is, the \emph{inverse} of \(\varepsilon\) in the \(\mathrm{hom}\)-direction (where
4685	  \(\varepsilon\) is invertible by
4686	  \cref{lem:isiso_eps_restrictscalars_appiso_hom}), \emph{not}
4687	  \(\varepsilon\) of the \(\mathrm{inv}\)-direction \(\beta\). (The reverse reindex turns an
4688	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(fW'')\)-section map into an \(\mathcal{O}_Y(W'')\)-map, so it must restrict
4689	  along the \(\mathrm{hom}\)-direction ring iso, whose codomain swap is therefore \(\mathtt{inv}\,
4690	  \varepsilon\) of \emph{that} functor.) The whole family is reindexed by the inverse of the
4691	  down-set bijection \(W'' \leftrightarrow f^{-1}W''\) above. The round-trip identities \(\mathtt{left\_inv}\)
4692	  and \(\mathtt{right\_inv}\) then close component-wise by \(\mathtt{Iso.inv\_hom\_id}\) and
4693	  \(\mathtt{Iso.hom\_inv\_id}\) of \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\) (the forward/inverse \(\beta\)-swaps
4694	  cancel) together with the cancellation of the down-set bijection
4695	  \(\iota_!(\iota^{-1}V) = V\) against its inverse.
4696	
4697	  For additivity and \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-linearity, one must be careful about \emph{which}
4698	  module structure carries the left-hand value. The additive and scalar structure on a dual
4699	  section of the left-hand value
4700	  \(\bigl((\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta).\mathtt{obj}\,(\mathtt{dual}\,M.\mathtt{val})\bigr)(V)\)
4701	  is the \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-module structure on the internal-hom object
4702	  (\cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom_slice_value}) --- the pointwise module structure in which the
4703	  sum and scalar multiple of dual sections are formed at the terminal object \(\mathrm{id}_{fV}\)
4704	  of the slice over \(fV\). This agrees with, but is \emph{a priori} a distinct presentation of,
4705	  the additive and scalar structure of the underlying presheaf morphism (the pointwise add and
4706	  scalar action on morphisms of presheaves of modules). The verification of additivity and
4707	  \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-linearity of the transport therefore proceeds in three distinct steps, the
4708	  first of which is a required first move rather than an afterthought:
4709	  \begin{enumerate}
4710	    \item[(i)] \emph{Identify the two module presentations.} The internal-hom module structure's
4711	      addition and scalar action are identified with the underlying pointwise add and scalar action
4712	      on presheaf morphisms. This is a definitional identification of the two
4713	      \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-module presentations of the same group of dual sections: the
4714	      internal-hom operations, formed at the terminal object of the slice, are precisely the
4715	      pointwise morphism-level operations of the presheaf morphism they package. Until this
4716	      identification is made, the additivity and scalar-compatibility obligations of the transport
4717	      cannot be read off the change-of-rings compatibility, because their sums and scalar multiples
4718	      are taken in the \emph{internal-hom} structure, not the morphism structure.
4719	    \item[(ii)] \emph{Match the X-side and Y-side scalars by the \(\beta\)-naturality ring
4720	      identity.} The scalar acting on the X-side (the pushforward-restricted scalar \(s\) by
4721	      which \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\beta_W\) multiplies the reindexed component) and the
4722	      scalar acting on the Y-side (the \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-scalar \(c\) of the internal-hom
4723	      action) are \emph{a priori} elements of different rings and cannot be compared directly.
4724	      The bridge is the ring identity
4725	      \[
4726	        s \;=\; (\beta.\mathtt{app}\,W').\mathrm{hom}\, c,
4727	      \]
4728	      obtained from \(\mathtt{InternalHom.termRingMap\_naturality}\) --- which records how the
4729	      internal-hom term-ring map transports the scalar action across the slice --- together with
4730	      the naturality of \(\beta\) on the thin poset \(\mathrm{Opens}\,Y\). Without this identity
4731	      the two sides of the scalar-compatibility equation are not even of matching type, so it
4732	      must be supplied before the change-of-rings compatibility of step (iii) can be invoked.
4733	      The restricted scalar action is made explicit by
4734	      \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars.smul\_def'}\), which exhibits the restricted smul
4735	      as the original smul along \(\beta_W\).
4736	    \item[(iii)] \emph{Apply the change-of-rings compatibility on the morphism level.} Once the
4737	      operations are expressed pointwise on the underlying morphisms and the scalars matched by
4738	      (ii), additivity and
4739	      \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-linearity follow from the post-composition compatibility of the
4740	      change-of-rings reindexing: the functor \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\beta_W\) preserves sums
4741	      and scalar multiples of morphisms, and the structure scalar is intertwined by the ring
4742	      isomorphism \(\beta_W\). This last is the presheaf-level shadow of

642	/-- **Restriction of a presheaf of modules to the over-category `Over U`.** This is
643	the `M|_U` of the slice internal-hom formula: `PresheafOfModules.pushforward₀` along
644	the over-category projection `Over.forget U`. The new base presheaf of rings is
645	`(Over.forget U).op ⋙ R`, whose value at the terminal `Over.mk (𝟙 U)` is `R(U)`. -/
646	noncomputable def restr (U : C)
647	    (M : PresheafOfModules.{u} (R ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)) :
648	    PresheafOfModules.{u} (((Over.forget U).op ⋙ R) ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat) :=
649	  (PresheafOfModules.pushforward₀ (Over.forget U) (R ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)).obj M

652	This is the `R(U)`-module underlying `ℋom(M, N)(U)` of blueprint
653	`def:presheaf_internal_hom`: the morphism group of the over-category restrictions
654	`M|_U ⟶ N|_U`, equipped with the `homModule` action for the terminal object
655	`Over.mk (𝟙 U)` of `Over U`. The full presheaf `internalHom` (the assembly of these
656	values over the restriction maps `V ⟶ U`) was assembled iter-220 (`internalHom`). -/
657	@[implicit_reducible]

659	    (M N : PresheafOfModules.{u} (R ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)) :
660	    Module (R.obj (Opposite.op U) : Type u) (restr U M ⟶ restr U N) :=
661	  homModule (R := (Over.forget U).op ⋙ R) (T := Over.mk (𝟙 U)) Over.mkIdTerminal
662	    (restr U M) (restr U N)

665	of `C`, restricting a morphism `φ : M|_U ⟶ N|_U` of restricted presheaves of modules
666	along `Over.map g : Over V ⥤ Over U` yields a morphism `M|_V ⟶ N|_V`. Realised as
667	`(pushforward₀ (Over.map g) …).map φ`; the target base ring presheaf
668	`(Over.map g).op ⋙ (Over.forget U).op ⋙ R` is definitionally `(Over.forget V).op ⋙ R`,
669	so the result has the expected type `restr V M ⟶ restr V N`. This is the
670	further-restriction map `φ ↦ φ|_V` of blueprint `lem:presheaf_internal_hom_restriction`. -/
671	noncomputable def restrictionMap {U V : C} (g : V ⟶ U)
672	    {M N : PresheafOfModules.{u} (R ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)}
673	    (φ : restr U M ⟶ restr U N) : restr V M ⟶ restr V N :=
674	  (PresheafOfModules.pushforward₀ (Over.map g)
675	    ((Over.forget U).op ⋙ (R ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat))).map φ
676

678	`lem:presheaf_internal_hom_restriction`. -/
679	lemma restrictionMap_add {U V : C} (g : V ⟶ U)
680	    {M N : PresheafOfModules.{u} (R ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)}
681	    (φ ψ : restr U M ⟶ restr U N) :
682	    restrictionMap g (φ + ψ) = restrictionMap g φ + restrictionMap g ψ := by
683	  ext1 X; rfl
684	
685	/-- **`restrictionMap` preserves zero.** -/
686	lemma restrictionMap_zero {U V : C} (g : V ⟶ U)
687	    {M N : PresheafOfModules.{u} (R ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)} :
688	    restrictionMap g (0 : restr U M ⟶ restr U N) = 0 := by
689	  ext1 X; rfl
690	
691	/-- Helper: the component of a morphism of presheaves of modules at two *equal*
692	objects agrees up to `HEq`. Proven by `subst`. Used to discharge the
693	pseudofunctoriality coherence of `restrictionMap` (`Over.map` is only functorial in
694	its argument up to `Over.mapId_eq` / `Over.mapComp_eq`). -/
695	private lemma hom_app_heq {B : Cᵒᵖ ⥤ RingCat.{u}} {M N : PresheafOfModules.{u} B}

697	  subst h; rfl

700	lemma restrictionMap_id {U : C}
701	    {M N : PresheafOfModules.{u} (R ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)}

703	    restrictionMap (𝟙 U) φ = φ := by
704	  ext1 X
705	  exact eq_of_heq (hom_app_heq φ (by rw [Over.mapId_eq]; rfl))

707	/-- **Functoriality of `restrictionMap`: composition.** For `g : V ⟶ U`, `h : W ⟶ V`,
708	restricting along `h ≫ g` is restricting along `g` then along `h`. -/
709	lemma restrictionMap_comp {U V W : C} (g : V ⟶ U) (h : W ⟶ V)
710	    {M N : PresheafOfModules.{u} (R ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)}
711	    (φ : restr U M ⟶ restr U N) :
712	    restrictionMap (h ≫ g) φ = restrictionMap h (restrictionMap g φ) := by
713	  ext1 X
714	  exact eq_of_heq (hom_app_heq φ (by rw [Over.mapComp_eq]; rfl))
715	
716	/-- **`restrictionMap` respects composition of morphisms** (functoriality of
717	`pushforward₀.map`). -/
718	lemma restrictionMap_comp_hom {U V : C} (g : V ⟶ U)
719	    {M N P : PresheafOfModules.{u} (R ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)}

721	    restrictionMap g (φ ≫ ψ) = restrictionMap g φ ≫ restrictionMap g ψ :=
722	  Functor.map_comp _ _ _
723	
724	/-- **Restriction commutes with the global-scalar endomorphism.** For `g : V ⟶ U`
725	and a global scalar `r ∈ R(U)`, restricting the multiplication-by-`r` endomorphism of
726	`N|_U` yields the multiplication-by-`R(g)(r)` endomorphism of `N|_V`. This is the heart
727	of the semilinearity of `restrictionMap` (blueprint `lem:presheaf_internal_hom_restriction`):
728	both sides act on each section by scalar multiplication, and the scalars agree by
729	functoriality of `R` (`termRingMap_naturality`). -/

732	    (r : (R.obj (Opposite.op U) : Type u)) :

735	      = globalSMul (R := (Over.forget V).op ⋙ R) (T := Over.mk (𝟙 V))
